preamble: The shift from the front vowel [æ] (or its lengthened variant [æː]) to a rhotic consonant [r] typically occurs as a form of hiatus resolution in non-rhotic English dialects. This is known as the 'intrusive R'.

examples.note: In dialects where final 'a' is pronounced closer to [æ], an intrusive R is inserted before a following vowel to prevent hiatus.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/accents-of-english/A9495FCF7CC13D1D11FADB3DE2F335F6
